lines: As by your high imperial Majesty
I had in charge at my depart for France,
As procurator to your Excellence,
To marry Princess Margaret for your Grace,
So, in the famous ancient city Tours,
In presence of the Kings of France and Sicil,
The Dukes of Orleance, Calaber, Britaigne, and
Alanson,
Seven earls, twelve barons, and twenty reverend
bishops,
I have performed my task and was espoused;
And humbly now upon my bended knee,
In sight of England and her lordly peers,
Deliver up my title in the Queen
To your most gracious hands, that are the substance
Of that great shadow I did represent:
The happiest gift that ever marquess gave,
The fairest queen that ever king received.
fullText: As by your high imperial Majesty I had in charge at my depart for France, As procurator to your Excellence, To marry Princess Margaret for your Grace, So, in the famous ancient city Tours, In presence of the Kings of France and Sicil, The Dukes of Orleance, Calaber, Britaigne, and Alanson, Seven earls, twelve barons, and twenty reverend bishops, I have performed my task and was espoused; And humbly now upon my bended knee, In sight of England and her lordly peers, Deliver up my title in the Queen To your most gracious hands, that are the substance Of that great shadow I did represent: The happiest gift that ever marquess gave, The fairest queen that ever king received.

lines: My Lord Protector, so it please your Grace,
Here are the articles of contracted peace
Between our sovereign and the French king Charles,
For eighteen months concluded by consent.
fullText: My Lord Protector, so it please your Grace, Here are the articles of contracted peace Between our sovereign and the French king Charles, For eighteen months concluded by consent.

lines: How now, fellow? Wouldst anything with
me?
fullText: How now, fellow? Wouldst anything with me?

lines: Thy wife too? That's some wrong indeed.--
What's yours? What's here?
the commons of Melford. How now, sir knave?
fullText: Thy wife too? That's some wrong indeed.-- What's yours? What's here? the commons of Melford. How now, sir knave?

lines: Who is there?
fullText: Who is there?

lines: Madam, be patient. As I was cause
Your Highness came to England, so will I
In England work your Grace's full content.
fullText: Madam, be patient. As I was cause Your Highness came to England, so will I In England work your Grace's full content.

lines: And he of these that can do most of all
Cannot do more in England than the Nevilles;
Salisbury and Warwick are no simple peers.
fullText: And he of these that can do most of all Cannot do more in England than the Nevilles; Salisbury and Warwick are no simple peers.

lines: Madam, myself have limed a bush for her
And placed a choir of such enticing birds
That she will light to listen to the lays
And never mount to trouble you again.
So let her rest. And, madam, list to me,
For I am bold to counsel you in this:
Although we fancy not the Cardinal,
Yet must we join with him and with the lords
Till we have brought Duke Humphrey in disgrace.
As for the Duke of York, this late complaint
Will make but little for his benefit.
So, one by one, we'll weed them all at last,
And you yourself shall steer the happy helm.
fullText: Madam, myself have limed a bush for her And placed a choir of such enticing birds That she will light to listen to the lays And never mount to trouble you again. So let her rest. And, madam, list to me, For I am bold to counsel you in this: Although we fancy not the Cardinal, Yet must we join with him and with the lords Till we have brought Duke Humphrey in disgrace. As for the Duke of York, this late complaint Will make but little for his benefit. So, one by one, we'll weed them all at last, And you yourself shall steer the happy helm.

lines: Resign it, then, and leave thine insolence.
Since thou wert king--as who is king but thou?--
The commonwealth hath daily run to wrack,
The Dauphin hath prevailed beyond the seas,
And all the peers and nobles of the realm
Have been as bondmen to thy sovereignty.
fullText: Resign it, then, and leave thine insolence. Since thou wert king--as who is king but thou?-- The commonwealth hath daily run to wrack, The Dauphin hath prevailed beyond the seas, And all the peers and nobles of the realm Have been as bondmen to thy sovereignty.

lines: Before we make election, give me leave
To show some reason, of no little force,
That York is most unmeet of any man.
fullText: Before we make election, give me leave To show some reason, of no little force, That York is most unmeet of any man.

lines: Peace, headstrong Warwick!
fullText: Peace, headstrong Warwick!

lines: Because here is a man accused of treason.
Pray God the Duke of York excuse himself!
fullText: Because here is a man accused of treason. Pray God the Duke of York excuse himself!

lines: Please it your Majesty, this is the man
That doth accuse his master of high treason.
His words were these: that Richard, Duke of York,
Was rightful heir unto the English crown,
And that your Majesty was an usurper.
fullText: Please it your Majesty, this is the man That doth accuse his master of high treason. His words were these: that Richard, Duke of York, Was rightful heir unto the English crown, And that your Majesty was an usurper.

lines: No marvel, an it like your Majesty,
My Lord Protector's hawks do tower so well;
They know their master loves to be aloft
And bears his thoughts above his falcon's pitch.
fullText: No marvel, an it like your Majesty, My Lord Protector's hawks do tower so well; They know their master loves to be aloft And bears his thoughts above his falcon's pitch.

lines: No malice, sir, no more than well becomes
So good a quarrel and so bad a peer.
fullText: No malice, sir, no more than well becomes So good a quarrel and so bad a peer.

lines: Why, as you, my lord,
An 't like your lordly Lord Protectorship.
fullText: Why, as you, my lord, An 't like your lordly Lord Protectorship.

lines: Come to the King, and tell him what miracle.
fullText: Come to the King, and tell him what miracle.

lines: What woman is this?
fullText: What woman is this?

lines: How cam'st thou so?
fullText: How cam'st thou so?

lines: And yet, I think, jet did he never see.
fullText: And yet, I think, jet did he never see.

lines: True, made the lame to leap and fly away.
fullText: True, made the lame to leap and fly away.

lines: Thus droops this lofty pine and hangs his sprays;
Thus Eleanor's pride dies in her youngest days.
fullText: Thus droops this lofty pine and hangs his sprays; Thus Eleanor's pride dies in her youngest days.

lines: Well hath your Highness seen into this duke,
And, had I first been put to speak my mind,
I think I should have told your Grace's tale.
The Duchess by his subornation,
Upon my life, began her devilish practices;
Or if he were not privy to those faults,
Yet, by reputing of his high descent--
As next the King he was successive heir,
And such high vaunts of his nobility--
Did instigate the bedlam brainsick duchess
By wicked means to frame our sovereign's fall.
Smooth runs the water where the brook is deep,
And in his simple show he harbors treason.
The fox barks not when he would steal the lamb.
No, no, my sovereign, Gloucester is a man
Unsounded yet and full of deep deceit.
fullText: Well hath your Highness seen into this duke, And, had I first been put to speak my mind, I think I should have told your Grace's tale. The Duchess by his subornation, Upon my life, began her devilish practices; Or if he were not privy to those faults, Yet, by reputing of his high descent-- As next the King he was successive heir, And such high vaunts of his nobility-- Did instigate the bedlam brainsick duchess By wicked means to frame our sovereign's fall. Smooth runs the water where the brook is deep, And in his simple show he harbors treason. The fox barks not when he would steal the lamb. No, no, my sovereign, Gloucester is a man Unsounded yet and full of deep deceit.

lines: Nay, Gloucester, know that thou art come too soon,
Unless thou wert more loyal than thou art.
I do arrest thee of high treason here.
fullText: Nay, Gloucester, know that thou art come too soon, Unless thou wert more loyal than thou art. I do arrest thee of high treason here.

lines: My lord, these faults are easy, quickly answered;
But mightier crimes are laid unto your charge
Whereof you cannot easily purge yourself.
I do arrest you in his Highness' name,
And here commit you to my Lord Cardinal
To keep until your further time of trial.
fullText: My lord, these faults are easy, quickly answered; But mightier crimes are laid unto your charge Whereof you cannot easily purge yourself. I do arrest you in his Highness' name, And here commit you to my Lord Cardinal To keep until your further time of trial.

lines: Hath he not twit our sovereign lady here
With ignominious words, though clerkly couched,
As if she had suborned some to swear
False allegations to o'erthrow his state?
fullText: Hath he not twit our sovereign lady here With ignominious words, though clerkly couched, As if she had suborned some to swear False allegations to o'erthrow his state?

lines: But, in my mind, that were no policy.
The King will labor still to save his life,
The Commons haply rise to save his life,
And yet we have but trivial argument,
More than mistrust, that shows him worthy death.
fullText: But, in my mind, that were no policy. The King will labor still to save his life, The Commons haply rise to save his life, And yet we have but trivial argument, More than mistrust, that shows him worthy death.

lines: Ah, York, no man alive so fain as I!
fullText: Ah, York, no man alive so fain as I!

lines: Madam, 'tis true; and were 't not madness then
To make the fox surveyor of the fold--
Who, being accused a crafty murderer,
His guilt should be but idly posted over
Because his purpose is not executed?
No, let him die in that he is a fox,
By nature proved an enemy to the flock,
Before his chaps be stained with crimson blood,
As Humphrey, proved by reasons, to my liege.
And do not stand on quillets how to slay him--
Be it by gins, by snares, by subtlety,
Sleeping or waking. 'Tis no matter how,
So he be dead; for that is good deceit
Which mates him first that first intends deceit.
fullText: Madam, 'tis true; and were 't not madness then To make the fox surveyor of the fold-- Who, being accused a crafty murderer, His guilt should be but idly posted over Because his purpose is not executed? No, let him die in that he is a fox, By nature proved an enemy to the flock, Before his chaps be stained with crimson blood, As Humphrey, proved by reasons, to my liege. And do not stand on quillets how to slay him-- Be it by gins, by snares, by subtlety, Sleeping or waking. 'Tis no matter how, So he be dead; for that is good deceit Which mates him first that first intends deceit.

lines: Not resolute, except so much were done,
For things are often spoke and seldom meant;
But that my heart accordeth with my tongue,
Seeing the deed is meritorious,
And to preserve my sovereign from his foe,
Say but the word and I will be his priest.
fullText: Not resolute, except so much were done, For things are often spoke and seldom meant; But that my heart accordeth with my tongue, Seeing the deed is meritorious, And to preserve my sovereign from his foe, Say but the word and I will be his priest.

lines: Here is my hand. The deed is worthy doing.
fullText: Here is my hand. The deed is worthy doing.

lines: Why, our authority is his consent,
And what we do establish he confirms.
Then, noble York, take thou this task in hand.
fullText: Why, our authority is his consent, And what we do establish he confirms. Then, noble York, take thou this task in hand.

lines: A charge, Lord York, that I will see performed.
But now return we to the false Duke Humphrey.
fullText: A charge, Lord York, that I will see performed. But now return we to the false Duke Humphrey.

lines: I'll see it truly done, my lord of York.
fullText: I'll see it truly done, my lord of York.

lines: Now, sirs, have you dispatched this thing?
fullText: Now, sirs, have you dispatched this thing?

lines: Why, that's well said. Go, get you to my house;
I will reward you for this venturous deed.
The King and all the peers are here at hand.
Have you laid fair the bed? Is all things well,
According as I gave directions?
fullText: Why, that's well said. Go, get you to my house; I will reward you for this venturous deed. The King and all the peers are here at hand. Have you laid fair the bed? Is all things well, According as I gave directions?

lines: Away, be gone.
fullText: Away, be gone.

lines: I'll call him presently, my noble lord.
fullText: I'll call him presently, my noble lord.

lines: Dead in his bed, my lord. Gloucester is dead.
fullText: Dead in his bed, my lord. Gloucester is dead.

lines: He doth revive again. Madam, be patient.
fullText: He doth revive again. Madam, be patient.

lines: Comfort, my sovereign! Gracious Henry, comfort!
fullText: Comfort, my sovereign! Gracious Henry, comfort!

lines: A dreadful oath, sworn with a solemn tongue!
What instance gives Lord Warwick for his vow?
fullText: A dreadful oath, sworn with a solemn tongue! What instance gives Lord Warwick for his vow?

lines: Why, Warwick, who should do the Duke to death?
Myself and Beaufort had him in protection,
And we, I hope, sir, are no murderers.
fullText: Why, Warwick, who should do the Duke to death? Myself and Beaufort had him in protection, And we, I hope, sir, are no murderers.

lines: I wear no knife to slaughter sleeping men,
But here's a vengeful sword, rusted with ease,
That shall be scoured in his rancorous heart
That slanders me with murder's crimson badge.--
Say, if thou dar'st, proud lord of Warwickshire,
That I am faulty in Duke Humphrey's death.
fullText: I wear no knife to slaughter sleeping men, But here's a vengeful sword, rusted with ease, That shall be scoured in his rancorous heart That slanders me with murder's crimson badge.-- Say, if thou dar'st, proud lord of Warwickshire, That I am faulty in Duke Humphrey's death.

lines: Blunt-witted lord, ignoble in demeanor!
If ever lady wronged her lord so much,
Thy mother took into her blameful bed
Some stern untutored churl, and noble stock
Was graft with crab-tree slip, whose fruit thou art
And never of the Nevilles' noble race.
fullText: Blunt-witted lord, ignoble in demeanor! If ever lady wronged her lord so much, Thy mother took into her blameful bed Some stern untutored churl, and noble stock Was graft with crab-tree slip, whose fruit thou art And never of the Nevilles' noble race.

lines: Thou shalt be waking while I shed thy blood,
If from this presence thou dar'st go with me.
fullText: Thou shalt be waking while I shed thy blood, If from this presence thou dar'st go with me.

lines: The trait'rous Warwick, with the men of Bury,
Set all upon me, mighty sovereign.
fullText: The trait'rous Warwick, with the men of Bury, Set all upon me, mighty sovereign.

lines: 'Tis like the Commons, rude unpolished hinds,
Could send such message to their sovereign!
employed,
To show how quaint an orator you are.
But all the honor Salisbury hath won
Is that he was the lord ambassador
Sent from a sort of tinkers to the King.
fullText: 'Tis like the Commons, rude unpolished hinds, Could send such message to their sovereign! employed, To show how quaint an orator you are. But all the honor Salisbury hath won Is that he was the lord ambassador Sent from a sort of tinkers to the King.

lines: Cease, gentle queen, these execrations,
And let thy Suffolk take his heavy leave.
fullText: Cease, gentle queen, these execrations, And let thy Suffolk take his heavy leave.

lines: A plague upon them! Wherefore should I curse
them?
Could curses kill, as doth the mandrake's groan,
I would invent as bitter searching terms,
As curst, as harsh, and horrible to hear,
Delivered strongly through my fixed teeth,
With full as many signs of deadly hate,
As lean-faced Envy in her loathsome cave.
My tongue should stumble in mine earnest words;
Mine eyes should sparkle like the beaten flint;
Mine hair be fixed on end, as one distract;
Ay, every joint should seem to curse and ban;
And even now my burdened heart would break
Should I not curse them. Poison be their drink!
Gall, worse than gall, the daintiest that they taste;
Their sweetest shade, a grove of cypress trees;
Their chiefest prospect, murd'ring basilisks;
Their softest touch, as smart as lizards' stings!
Their music, frightful as the serpent's hiss,
And boding screech owls make the consort full!
All the foul terrors in dark-seated hell--
fullText: A plague upon them! Wherefore should I curse them? Could curses kill, as doth the mandrake's groan, I would invent as bitter searching terms, As curst, as harsh, and horrible to hear, Delivered strongly through my fixed teeth, With full as many signs of deadly hate, As lean-faced Envy in her loathsome cave. My tongue should stumble in mine earnest words; Mine eyes should sparkle like the beaten flint; Mine hair be fixed on end, as one distract; Ay, every joint should seem to curse and ban; And even now my burdened heart would break Should I not curse them. Poison be their drink! Gall, worse than gall, the daintiest that they taste; Their sweetest shade, a grove of cypress trees; Their chiefest prospect, murd'ring basilisks; Their softest touch, as smart as lizards' stings! Their music, frightful as the serpent's hiss, And boding screech owls make the consort full! All the foul terrors in dark-seated hell--

lines: You bade me ban, and will you bid me leave?
Now, by the ground that I am banished from,
Well could I curse away a winter's night,
Though standing naked on a mountain top
Where biting cold would never let grass grow,
And think it but a minute spent in sport.
fullText: You bade me ban, and will you bid me leave? Now, by the ground that I am banished from, Well could I curse away a winter's night, Though standing naked on a mountain top Where biting cold would never let grass grow, And think it but a minute spent in sport.

lines: Thus is poor Suffolk ten times banished,
Once by the King, and three times thrice by thee.
'Tis not the land I care for, wert thou thence.
A wilderness is populous enough,
So Suffolk had thy heavenly company;
For where thou art, there is the world itself,
With every several pleasure in the world;
And where thou art not, desolation.
I can no more. Live thou to joy thy life;
Myself no joy in naught but that thou liv'st.
fullText: Thus is poor Suffolk ten times banished, Once by the King, and three times thrice by thee. 'Tis not the land I care for, wert thou thence. A wilderness is populous enough, So Suffolk had thy heavenly company; For where thou art, there is the world itself, With every several pleasure in the world; And where thou art not, desolation. I can no more. Live thou to joy thy life; Myself no joy in naught but that thou liv'st.

lines: If I depart from thee, I cannot live;
And in thy sight to die, what were it else
But like a pleasant slumber in thy lap?
Here could I breathe my soul into the air,
As mild and gentle as the cradle babe
Dying with mother's dug between its lips;
Where, from thy sight, I should be raging mad
And cry out for thee to close up mine eyes,
To have thee with thy lips to stop my mouth.
So shouldst thou either turn my flying soul,
Or I should breathe it so into thy body,
And then it lived in sweet Elysium.
To die by thee were but to die in jest;
From thee to die were torture more than death.
O, let me stay, befall what may befall!
fullText: If I depart from thee, I cannot live; And in thy sight to die, what were it else But like a pleasant slumber in thy lap? Here could I breathe my soul into the air, As mild and gentle as the cradle babe Dying with mother's dug between its lips; Where, from thy sight, I should be raging mad And cry out for thee to close up mine eyes, To have thee with thy lips to stop my mouth. So shouldst thou either turn my flying soul, Or I should breathe it so into thy body, And then it lived in sweet Elysium. To die by thee were but to die in jest; From thee to die were torture more than death. O, let me stay, befall what may befall!

lines: I go.
fullText: I go.

lines: A jewel locked into the woefull'st cask
That ever did contain a thing of worth!
Even as a splitted bark, so sunder we.
This way fall I to death.
fullText: A jewel locked into the woefull'st cask That ever did contain a thing of worth! Even as a splitted bark, so sunder we. This way fall I to death.

lines: Look on my George; I am a gentleman.
Rate me at what thou wilt, thou shalt be paid.
fullText: Look on my George; I am a gentleman. Rate me at what thou wilt, thou shalt be paid.

lines: Thy name affrights me, in whose sound is death.
A cunning man did calculate my birth
And told me that by water I should die.
Yet let not this make thee be bloody-minded;
Thy name is Gualtier, being rightly sounded.
fullText: Thy name affrights me, in whose sound is death. A cunning man did calculate my birth And told me that by water I should die. Yet let not this make thee be bloody-minded; Thy name is Gualtier, being rightly sounded.

lines: Stay, Whitmore, for thy prisoner is a prince,
The Duke of Suffolk, William de la Pole.
fullText: Stay, Whitmore, for thy prisoner is a prince, The Duke of Suffolk, William de la Pole.

lines: Ay, but these rags are no part of the Duke.
Jove sometimes went disguised, and why not I?
fullText: Ay, but these rags are no part of the Duke. Jove sometimes went disguised, and why not I?

lines: Obscure and lousy swain, King Henry's blood,
The honorable blood of Lancaster,
Must not be shed by such a jaded groom.
Hast thou not kissed thy hand and held my stirrup?
Bareheaded plodded by my footcloth mule,
And thought thee happy when I shook my head?
How often hast thou waited at my cup,
Fed from my trencher, kneeled down at the board,
When I have feasted with Queen Margaret?
Remember it, and let it make thee crestfall'n,
Ay, and allay this thy abortive pride.
How in our voiding lobby hast thou stood
And duly waited for my coming forth?
This hand of mine hath writ in thy behalf,
And therefore shall it charm thy riotous tongue.
fullText: Obscure and lousy swain, King Henry's blood, The honorable blood of Lancaster, Must not be shed by such a jaded groom. Hast thou not kissed thy hand and held my stirrup? Bareheaded plodded by my footcloth mule, And thought thee happy when I shook my head? How often hast thou waited at my cup, Fed from my trencher, kneeled down at the board, When I have feasted with Queen Margaret? Remember it, and let it make thee crestfall'n, Ay, and allay this thy abortive pride. How in our voiding lobby hast thou stood And duly waited for my coming forth? This hand of mine hath writ in thy behalf, And therefore shall it charm thy riotous tongue.

lines: Base slave, thy words are blunt, and so art thou.
fullText: Base slave, thy words are blunt, and so art thou.

lines: Thou dar'st not for thy own.
fullText: Thou dar'st not for thy own.

lines: Pole!
fullText: Pole!

lines: O, that I were a god, to shoot forth thunder
Upon these paltry, servile, abject drudges!
Small things make base men proud. This villain
here,
Being captain of a pinnace, threatens more
Than Bargulus, the strong Illyrian pirate.
Drones suck not eagles' blood, but rob beehives.
It is impossible that I should die
By such a lowly vassal as thyself.
Thy words move rage and not remorse in me.
I go of message from the Queen to France.
I charge thee waft me safely cross the Channel.
fullText: O, that I were a god, to shoot forth thunder Upon these paltry, servile, abject drudges! Small things make base men proud. This villain here, Being captain of a pinnace, threatens more Than Bargulus, the strong Illyrian pirate. Drones suck not eagles' blood, but rob beehives. It is impossible that I should die By such a lowly vassal as thyself. Thy words move rage and not remorse in me. I go of message from the Queen to France. I charge thee waft me safely cross the Channel.

lines: Paene gelidus timor occupat artus.
It is thee I fear.
fullText: Paene gelidus timor occupat artus. It is thee I fear.

lines: Suffolk's imperial tongue is stern and rough,
Used to command, untaught to plead for favor.
Far be it we should honor such as these
With humble suit. No, rather let my head
Stoop to the block than these knees bow to any
Save to the God of heaven and to my king;
And sooner dance upon a bloody pole
Than stand uncovered to the vulgar groom.
True nobility is exempt from fear.--
More can I bear than you dare execute.
fullText: Suffolk's imperial tongue is stern and rough, Used to command, untaught to plead for favor. Far be it we should honor such as these With humble suit. No, rather let my head Stoop to the block than these knees bow to any Save to the God of heaven and to my king; And sooner dance upon a bloody pole Than stand uncovered to the vulgar groom. True nobility is exempt from fear.-- More can I bear than you dare execute.

lines: Come, soldiers, show what cruelty you can,
That this my death may never be forgot!
Great men oft die by vile bezonians:
A Roman sworder and banditto slave
Murdered sweet Tully; Brutus' bastard hand
Stabbed Julius Caesar; savage islanders
Pompey the Great, and Suffolk dies by pirates.
Suffolk and Others.]
fullText: Come, soldiers, show what cruelty you can, That this my death may never be forgot! Great men oft die by vile bezonians: A Roman sworder and banditto slave Murdered sweet Tully; Brutus' bastard hand Stabbed Julius Caesar; savage islanders Pompey the Great, and Suffolk dies by pirates. Suffolk and Others.]
